policy_statement: Preserve and produce 135,000 housing units (including 60,000 affordable units) by 2027, with 75% of new housing located within 1/2 mile of Imagine Austin activity centers and corridors.
mechanism_description: Concentrating new housing development near activity centers and corridors enables shorter trips and greater transit/walking use, reducing vehicle travel and associated emissions while supporting more compact land use.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Compact, infill-oriented growth reduces per-capita land consumption and typically reduces infrastructure and energy/service inputs (e.g., shorter trip lengths and more efficient shared networks) for delivering the same urban functions.
primary_causal_pathway: Land-use + mobility system planning → mode shift/shorter trips (↓ VMT, ↑ transit/walk/bike) → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: City land-use decisions (zoning, siting targets, permitting, incentives) steer new housing/jobs/non-residential growth into activity centers and along transit corridors and improve transit performance, enabling higher-density mixed-use development near stations/stops.  
2) Mediating physical process: Increased proximity and accessibility reduce trip distances and make non-auto modes feasible; households and firms substitute away from private car travel, lowering total VMT and gasoline/diesel combustion.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced tailpipe CO₂ (and associated upstream fuel-cycle emissions), with additional potential reductions from avoided sprawl (less land conversion and lower per-capita infrastructure/material demand).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced transportation GHG emissions due to lower private vehicle use and VMT from concentrating development in transit-served, walkable centers/corridors.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Land-use concentration near high-performing transit reduces car use/VMT, directly reducing transportation fuel combustion and GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While TOD can also deliver co-benefits (e.g., reduced infrastructure needs, potential heat-island or air-quality benefits), the mechanism’s defining and largest climate effect is transportation emissions reduction via mode shift and trip shortening—therefore Mitigation is primary.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary:  
   - Resource Efficiency: Compact development inherently uses land more efficiently and tends to reduce per-capita network infrastructure and travel energy inputs for the same access to jobs/services.  
4) Edge cases applied: If a “TOD” policy is implemented without real transit improvement or without enabling density/mixed-use (e.g., nominal station-area branding), emissions benefits may be smaller, but the mechanism as defined still targets VMT reduction as the primary climate effect.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any genuine TOD/siting-to-centers policy aims to shift travel patterns away from driving and reduce sprawl; these consistently map to Mitigation as primary, with Resource Efficiency as an inherent co-effect of compactness.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If station-area growth induces significant additional travel demand (e.g., regional in-commuting by car due to insufficient last-mile access/parking policies) or causes displacement that lengthens commutes elsewhere, realized mitigation can vary; however, these are implementation/outcome contingencies rather than a different underlying mechanism.
key_indicators: “By 2027”; “preserve and produce 135,000 housing units”; “including 60,000 affordable housing units”; “75% of new housing located within ½ mile of…activity centers and corridors”
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: improved housing affordability/access; reduced household transportation costs; potential reductions in heat exposure and emissions co-pollutants through reduced VMT; potential reduction in greenfield land conversion (ecosystem benefits). Not sufficient to code an additional secondary without explicit hazard-focused provisions.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Within ½ mile of…corridors” could overlap with flood-prone creek corridors in Austin, but the text does not specify floodplain avoidance or resilient siting; therefore adaptation classification is not added.
